main.content: {{inscription |reading=mine viku muluveneke!muluevneke arp̣aś kamaia |reading_lepontic={{c|A|A14}}{{c|I}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|M}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|K|K4}}{{c|Ś|Ś2}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|P}}{{c|R|R5}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|E}}{{c|K|K4}}{{c|E}}{{c|N}}{{c|V}}{{c|E}}{{c|U}}{{c|L}}{{c|U}}{{c|M}}{{c|U}}{{c|K|K4}}{{c|I}}{{c|V}}{{c|E}}{{c|N}}{{c|I}}{{c|M}} |direction=sinistroverse |letter_height_min=2.5 cm |letter_number_min=29 |word_number=5 |line_number=1 |corpus=Etruscan |script=Etruscan script |object=Cl 3.2 Chiusi |position=shoulder, outside |orientation=0 |workmanship=scratched after firing |condition=complete |type_inscription=dedicatory |language=Etruscan |meaning='Viku gave me to Arpa Kamai' |source_detail=Morandi 2004: 573 n. 33 |checklevel=2 |problem=Literatur, Schreibung, was is das andere ET-Ding das Morandi erwähnt (ET II p. 330)? }} ==Commentary== Images in {{bib|Cristofani 1977}}: tav. XXXIb and c, XXXIIa (photos). Inscribed on the shoulder of the olla just below the rim; well legible except for pi, which is damaged by a break, but clear. The letters are executed by a different hand than the decoration, and with some uncertainty: the waw {{c||V}} of {{w||viku}} was begun as an alpha {{c||A14}}, and waw and epsilon in ''muluevneke'' are transposed ({{bib|Cristofani 1977}}: 198). Reading and interpretation according to {{bib|Cristofani 1977}}: 198 f., who follows Colonna's suggestion (at a conference) to identify {{w||viku}} as the personal name of the subject and donor of the gift. Cristofani merely identifies the name as belonging to a group of "Individualnamen" in -''u'' attested in the 7th century. It is classified as Celtic and compared with putative {{w||viχu}} ([[VA·4.2]]) by {{bib|Morandi 2004}}: 573, n. 33. While it is not clear what a random Celt was doing in 7th-century Chiusi, giving vases to Etruscan ladies, Etruscan comparanda for the base are absent (according to the index of {{bib|ET²}}). See also '''{{bib|CIE}} 3234''', '''{{bib|TLE}} 481'''.

==Commentary==
{{w||celθestra}} is suggested to be derived from a Greek *''κελτός'' by '''{{bib|Colonna 1985}}: 270 f.'''; see the word page.
{{bibliography}}

{{inscription
|reading=!] aviles katacinas
|reading_lepontic={{c|S}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|N}}{{c|I}}{{c|K|K5}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|T|T2}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|K|K5}}{{c|S||d}}{{c|E}}{{c|L}}{{c|I}}{{c|V}}{{c|A|A14}}[
|direction=sinistroverse
|letter_height_min=5 cm
|letter_height_max=7 cm
|letter_number_min=15
|word_number=3
|line_number=1
|corpus=Etruscan
|script=Etruscan script
|object=Vs 1.165 Orvieto
|position=front
|orientation=0
|workmanship=carved
|condition=incomplete
|type_inscription=funerary
|language=Etruscan
|meaning='... of Avile Katakina'
|source_detail=De Simone 1978b: 370–395
|checklevel=3
|problem=Zeichenformen nach der ungenauen Zeichnung in Bonamici, De Simone in Etr/Kat 135
}}
==Commentary==
Image in {{bib|Bonamici et al. 1994}}: 41, fig. 12 (drawing), 43, fig. 14 (drawing).

Inscribed across four slabs on the front wall of the tomb, above the entrance (length of inscription 164 cm according to {{bib|Cristofani 1980}}: 337); the first part (''mi'' 'I (am)') is missing.

The name underlying the Etruscan patronym/gentilicium in -''na'' {{w||katakinas}} (genitive) is argued to be etymologically Celtic by {{bib|De Simone 1978b}}; see further on the word page.

See also {{bib|Vitali 1998}}: 257.
{{bibliography}}

==Commentary==
The name underlying the Etruscan patronym/gentilicium in -''na'' {{w||vercenas}} (genitive) is argued to be etymologically Celtic by {{bib|De Simone 1978b}}; see further on the word page.

See also '''{{bib|CIE}}: 4991''', {{bib|Vitali 1998}}: 257.
{{bibliography}}

{{inscription
|reading=mi suθi larθial muθikuś
|reading_lepontic={{c|Ś|Ś2}}{{c|U}}{{c|K|K4}}{{c|I}}{{c|Θ}}{{c|U}}{{c|M}}{{c|L}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|I}}{{c|Θ}}{{c|R}}{{c|A|A14}}{{c|L}}{{c|I}}{{c|Θ}}{{c|U}}{{c|S}}{{c|I}}{{c|M}}
|direction=sinistroverse
|letter_height_min=0
|letter_number_min=20
|word_number=4
|line_number=1
|corpus=Etruscan
|script=Etruscan script
|object=Li 1.1 Busca
|position=front
|frame_left=straight
|frame_middle=top and bottom
|frame_right=straight
|workmanship=carved
|condition=complete
|type_inscription=funerary
|language=Etruscan
|meaning='I (am) the grave of Larθ Muθiku'
|source_detail=Colonna 1998: 261–264
|checklevel=5
|problem=literature, Schreibung th aber k
}}
==Commentary==
First published in {{bib|Durandi 1774}}: 129 f.
 
Images in {{bib|Durandi 1774}}: 130 (drawing), {{bib|CII}}: tav. V (drawing), {{bib|Gambari & Colonna 1988}}: tav. XLIX (photo), {{bib|Colonna 1998}}: 262, fig. 244 (drawing = {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 337, fig. 12).

Inscribed on a flattened side of the rough stone, in a horseshoe-shaped frame, which documents the transmission of this Etruscan style from the region of Volterra to the north ({{bib|Gambari & Colonna 1988}}: 152, {{bib|Colonna 1998}}: 262, cf. [[VA·6]]).

The first name is the standard Etruscan praenomen ''larθ'' (in the genitive ''larθial''); the second name {{w||muθikuś}} is compared to Gaulish names like ''moticius'' by {{bib|Gambari & Colonna 1988}}: 154, though they refer to Larθ Muθiku as a Ligurian (also {{bib|Colonna 1998}}: 261 "celto-ligure", {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 337 f.). IE /{{p||o}}/ is spelled with upsilon in the Etruscan alphabet. See further on the word page.

{{bib|Colonna 1998}}: 261–264 suggests that a "Celto-Ligurian" by the name of *''motiko'' lived in the region of Volterra, where he took the Etruscan name ''larθ'', while his Ligurian name functioned as gentilicium. Buried in his putative native country, his Etruscan-style epitaph was written on a local type of monument.

*{{bib|NRIE}}: 5
*{{bib|TLE}}: 721
*{{bib|Aigner Foresti 1985}}: 3-11
*{{bib|Verger 2001}}: 279 f. compares letter forms to those in [[JU·1]], 284
*G. Buonamici, Epigraphia Etrusca, Firenze 1932 (pl. 19, 30)

See also {{bib|Mommsen 1853}}: 215 f., {{bib|CII}}: 7, no. 42 with older literature, {{bib|Tibiletti Bruno 1968d}}: 265.
{{bibliography}}

{{inscription |reading=mi nemetieś!nemetịẹś |reading_lepontic={{c|Ś|Ś2}}{{c|E}}{{c|I}}{{c|T|T9|d}}{{c|E}}{{c|M}}{{c|E}}{{c|N}}{{c|I}}{{c|M}} |direction=sinistroverse |letter_height_min=5.5 cm |letter_height_max=6 cm |letter_number_min=10 |word_number=2 |line_number=1 |corpus=Etruscan |script=Etruscan script |object=Li 1.2 Genova |position=front |orientation=270 |workmanship=carved |condition=complete |type_inscription=unknown |language=Etruscan |meaning='I (am) of Nemetie' |sortdate=-475 |date=first half of 5th c. BC |date_derivation‎=palaeography |source_detail=Bermond Montanari 1980: 296 f. |checklevel=1 |problem=mehr Literatur? }} ==Commentary== Images in {{bib|Bermond Montanari 1980}}: 296 (drawing = {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 338, fig. 13), {{bib|Bermond Montanari 1980b}}: LXXXVII a and b (photos), {{bib|Arcà & Rubat Borel 2024}}: 37, fig. 10 (photo). Inscribed from the bottom toward the top on the face side and then around the top of the ciottolone (length 37.5 cm); due to surface abrasion on this extremity, the reading of iota and epsilon is uncertain ({{bib|Bermond Montanari 1980}}: 296). {{bib|Bermond Montanari 1980}}: 296 dates the inscription palaeographically to the first half of the 5th century BC; {{bib|De Simone 1980}}: 198 adds that no syncope has occurred in {{w||nemetieś}}. The function of the inscription appears to be unclear – {{bib|Bermond Montanari 1980}}: 296 considers it to be funerary, {{bib|De Simone 1980}}: 198 an owner's inscription, {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 338 a guarantee of weight. The personal name {{w||nemetieś}} is noted to be "riscontrabile in area gallo-ligure" by {{bib|Bermond Montanari 1980}}: 296, who compares Gaul. ''νεμητον'' and personal names in ''nam''(''et'')-. More precise {{bib|De Simone 1980}}: 198 f., who identifies the name as a loan from Gaulish; see further on the word page. {{bib|Colonna 2004}}: 303 f. reports an additional character on the back of the stone, resembling a sinistroverse ligature of tau {{c||T2}} and epsilon {{c||E9}}. See also {{bib|Maggiani 1987}}: 439.

{{inscription
|reading=mini muluvanike!mu?uvanike keivale
|reading_lepontic={{c|E}}{{c|L}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|V}}{{c|I}}{{c|E}}{{c|K}}{{c|E}}{{c|K}}{{c|I}}{{c|N}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|V}}{{c|U}}?{{c|U}}{{c|M}}{{c|I}}{{c|N}}{{c|I}}{{c|M}}
|direction=sinistroverse
|letter_height_min=0.6 cm
|letter_height_max=0.8 cm
|letter_number_min=21
|word_number=3
|line_number=1
|corpus=Etruscan
|script=Etruscan script
|object=AS 3.1 Castelnuovo Berardenga
|position=outside, wall
|orientation=0
|frame_left=none
|frame_middle=top and bottom
|frame_right=none
|workmanship=scratched into leather-hard clay
|condition=complete, damaged
|type_inscription=dedicatory
|language=Etruscan
|meaning='Keivale gave me'
|source_detail=Mangani 1986: 277–279
|checklevel=2
|problem={{bib|CIE}}: 302, {{bib|NRIE}}: 1159, {{bib|TLE}}: 734 kann alles nicht sein wegen Funddatum
}}
==Commentary==
Images in {{bib|Mangani 1986}}: 278 (drawing) and tav. XLII.3 (photo).

Inscribed below the rim of the cup between the two handles; well legible despite breaks and surface damage effacing the lambda of {{w||muluvanike}}. Measurements from {{bib|Mangani 1986}}: 278, no. 2. Mangani says that the decoration was made before firing, which seems to include the inscription. Cf. the similar inscription [[AS 3.2]] on an identical object from the same grave.

The text is a standard Etruscan dedicatory speaking inscription. The personal name {{w||keivale}} is grammatically Etruscan, but is suggested to be "di origine forse non etrusca" by {{bib|Mangani 1986}}: 278 on account of the apparent suffix -''al''-, which she associates with Raetic, Ligurian and/or Lepontic; see further on the word page.

See also {{bib|Gambari & Colonna 1988}}: 154, {{bib|Colonna 1998}}: 263, {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 338.

*Stephanie A. Layton, Etruscan Bucchero Pottery from Cetamura del Chianti (Gaiole), in Etruscan Studies 12.1 (2009)
{{bibliography}}

{{inscription |reading=eluveitie |reading_lepontic={{c|E|E3}}{{c|I}}{{c|T|T5}}{{c|I}}{{c|E|E3}}{{c|V|V3}}{{c|U}}{{c|L}}{{c|E|E3}} |direction=sinistroverse |letter_height_min=0 |letter_number_min=9 |word_number=1 |line_number=1 |corpus=Etruscan |script=Etruscan script |object=Pa 0.3 Mantova |position=inside, bottom |orientation=0 |workmanship=scratched after firing |condition=complete |type_inscription=unknown |language=Etruscan |meaning='Eluveitie' |source_detail=Vitali & Kaenel 2000: 115–122 |checklevel=0 }} ==Commentary== First published in {{bib|De Marinis 1987b}}: 131. Images in {{bib|De Marinis 1987b}}: 131, fig. 124 (photo = {{bib|Vitali 1998}}: fig. 4 = {{bib|Vitali & Kaenel 2000}}: 118, fig. 2), {{bib|De Marinis 1989}}: 43, fig. 21.6 (drawing = {{bib|Vitali 1998}}: fig. 4 = {{bib|Vitali & Kaenel 2000}}: 118, fig. 2 = {{bib|Sassatelli 2003}}: 240 = {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 333, fig. 7 = {{bib|De Marinis 2005}}: 74, fig. 17.1). Inscribed on the inside of the bowl along the wall above the foot. The reading is unproblematic; the alphabet used it that of other Etruscan inscriptions from Mantova and Forcello ({{bib|Pandolfini 1986}}, {{bib|De Marinis 2005}}), indeed the letter forms are exactly the same as those in the alphabetarium from Castellazzo della Garolda (4th c. BC; {{bib|De Marinis 2005}}: 58, fig. 20), inluding epsilon and waw with their hastae not prolonged beneath the lowest bar, waw with the bars far apart, and tau with the bar not crossing the hasta. The form is an Etruscan name with the typical auslaut -''ie'' of names borrowed from IE ''i̯o''-stems in the vocative (see the word page). The base is Celtic *''eluφeitii̯os'' as in the ethnonym 'Helvetian' ({{bib|Vitali 1998}}: 262–264), though we cannot be certain whether the Etruscan attestation is a cognomen/nickname literally meaning 'the Helvetian' ({{bib|Sassatelli 2003}}: 240 = {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 333), an individual name, or a nomen, and thus whether the person who bore it actually identified as Helvetian and was a speaker of a Celtic language. Cf. {{w||kalatiknos}} for another ethnonym-derived name of similarly uncertain status. The historical background for a Helvetian in Mantova is discussed by {{bib|Vitali & Kaenel 2000}}: 115–122. The inscription was originally given the siglum MN·2 in LexLep, but is now filed under its Etruscan siglum Pa 0.3 after being added to the Etruscan corpus in {{bib|ET²}}. See also {{bib|De Marinis 1989}}: 40 with n. 37, {{bib|De Marinis 2005}}: 75.

{{inscription
|reading=kuvei puleisnai mi iśive !miśe[ space !]ḳṣ́[ space !]ẹṇḳe zilaθ miśalalati amake
|reading_lepontic={{c|E}}{{c|K|K4}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|M}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|I}}{{c|T|T9|d}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|L}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|L}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|S}}{{c|I}}{{c|M}}{{c|T}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|L}}{{c|I}}{{c|Z|Z6}}{{c|E}}{{c|k|K4}}{{c|N}}{{c|E}}[    ]??[    ]{{c|E}}?{{c|I}}{{c|M}}{{c|E}}{{c|V}}{{c|I}}?{{c|I}}{{c|I}}{{c|M}}{{c|I}}{{c|A|A24}}{{c|N}}{{c|S|S|d}}{{c|I}}{{c|E}}{{c|L}}{{c|U}}{{c|P|P2}}{{c|I}}{{c|E}}{{c|V}}{{c|U}}{{c|K|K4}}
|direction=sinistroverse
|letter_height_min=3 cm
|letter_number_min=44
|word_number=0
|line_number=1
|corpus=Etruscan
|script=Etruscan script
|object=Pa 1.2 Rubiera
|position=centre
|orientation=0
|frame_middle=top and bottom
|workmanship=carved
|condition=incomplete
|type_inscription=funerary
|language=Etruscan
|meaning='Kuvei Puleisnai ...'
|source_detail=De Simone 1992b: 11 f.
|checklevel=4
|problem=P, T, San, more literature?
}}
==Commentary==
First published in {{bib|Bermond Montanari 1988}}.

Images in {{bib|Bermond Montanari 1988}}: 241, fig. 2 (drawings), 243, fig. 3 (above, drawing = {{bib|Malnati & Bermond Montanari 1989}}: 1575, fig. 5 = {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 325, fig. 2), tav. LXIII.35 (photo).

Inscribed in a single band around the circumference of the cippus, separating two decorative panels. Numerous letters are damaged, especially in a section of ca. 30 cm around the middle part.

The personal name {{w||kuvei}} is interpreted by '''{{bib|De Simone 1992b}}: 11 f.''' as the Etruscan feminine form in -''i'' of an etymologically Celtic name ''kou̯os''; see further on the word page.

See also {{bib|Vitali 1998}}: 257, {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 335.
{{bibliography}}

{{inscription |reading=? χuiiie!χuiiie |reading_lepontic={{c||line_d2_1}}{{c|Ψ||d}}{{c|U||d}}{{c|I||d}}{{c|I||d}}{{c|I||d}}{{c|E||d}} |reading_variant=?{{w|58}}{{w|e (word)|e}}
{{c|line_d2_1}}{{c|50 (character)|50 (character)2|d}}{{c|5 (character)||d}}{{c|1 (character)||d}}{{c|1 (character)||d}}{{c|1 (character)||d}}{{c||E|d}} |direction=ambiguous |letter_height_min=0.8 cm |letter_height_max=1.2 cm |letter_number_min=6 |word_number=1 |line_number=1 |corpus=Etruscan |script=Etruscan script |script_certainty=perh. |object=SH·1 Pansdorf |position=rim |workmanship=chased |condition=complete |type_inscription=unknown |language=Etruscan |linguistic_ascription=perhaps |meaning=unknown |source_detail=Mees 2020: 175–182 |checklevel=0 }} == Commentary == First published in {{bib|Stjernquist 1965}}. First interpreted as Cisalpine Celtic by {{bib|Mees 2020}}. Images in {{bib|Stjernquist 1965}}: Taf. XV,4 (photo = {{bib|Stjernquist 1967}}: Taf. XLII,2b), {{bib|Mees 2020}}: 176, fig. 1 (photo). The marks are embossed, with a depth of ca. 1 mm, from above on the rim of the cist near one of the handle attachments (displayed above as seen from the inside). They were rarely referred to in the older literature and were only given attention upon being re-discovered after the cist was retrieved from the burned cathedral museum. They were first described in detail by {{bib|Stjernquist 1965}}: 119, who booked the presence of an inscription as one of the similarities of the cist with the Ticino group (see [[SH·1 Pansdorf]]), assuming that such marks were typical for the Alpine area and specifically the Ticino (p. 123 f.). Stjernquist consulted G. B. Pellegrini, whose question-mark-studded assessment based on a photograph was that the sequence {{c||Ψ|d}}{{c||U|d}}{{c||I|d}}{{c||I|d}}{{c||I|d}} could be a number '58' and the epsilon at the end the abbreviation of a unit of measurement (p. 124, {{bib|Stjernquist 1967}}: 135). Pellegrini ignored what he thought to be two oblique strokes before {{c||Ψ|d}}, judging them to be unlikely to be alphabetical or numerical signs (without reasoning); the leftmost one is in fact a break in the rim ({{bib|Stjernquist 1967}}: 135, n. 6), but the one immediately next to {{c||Ψ|d}} is intentional. Stjernquist assumed the number to be a measure of capacity referring to the volume of the cist. An analysis of the character sequence as a language-encoding inscription is put forward by {{bib|Mees 2020}}, who argues that "a lexical reading for a relatively long text is much better paralleled for a bronzeware vessel of this date" (p. 176): in the absence of languge-encoding inscriptions on cists from Cisalpine Celtic context, Mees compares the inscriptions on bronze Schnabelkannen [[GR·3]] and [[TI·18]]. Based on Stjernquist's association of the object with the Ticino, Mees interprets the sequence as a Celtic name ''koii̯os'' adapted to Etruscan {{w||χuiiie|kuii̯e}} (see [https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/Raetic_onomastics TIR] for the adaptation of IE names in Tyrsenian languages). Pointing to the Etruscoid earliest documents in the Lepontic area, Mees suggests that the alphabet is a sort of proto-Etrusco-Lepontic, in which the employment of upsilon for /o/ is an Etruscan feature and the use of chi for /g/ was not yet fixed. For the spelling of triple iota for ''ii̯'', he compares the Venetic inscription Es 31 which contains a form ]'''biiiako.s.''' ''bii̯akos'', in which the offset first iota represents the vowel and the following two iotas the glide. Mees assumes that Kuii̯e is the name of the vessel's manufacturer (p. 180) and dates object and inscription to the 6th century based on the form of chi (p. 177). While Mees' analysis of the sequence is not impossible, there are a number of issues. The first and gravest one is the unmotivated exclusion of the oblique stroke before {{c||Ψ}}, which according to Mees "does not make connection with the next part of the text", but is in fact just as close to {{c||Ψ}} as the following stroke. Both Pellegrini and Mees offer no explanation for its presence and exclude it from their readings purely because it cannot be read as a character and disturbs the otherwise feasible interpretations. A further problem is the explanation of the three consecutive strokes, which would generally be considered a clear indication of non-lexical content and probably prompted Pellegrini's numeral interpretation, as an over-zealous representation of vowel plus glide. The Venetic comparandum is valid, but weak because it is an unicum – in a text whose lexicality is beyond doubt, a linguistic explanation can be made plausible, but in a context where we do not expect a language-encoding inscription anyway, such an unusual spelling is not quite convincing. Embossed marks on the rims of ribbed pails which can be compared to those on the Pansdorf cist are:
*from Etruscan context {{bib|Stjernquist 1967}} no. 38 (Monteveglio, {{c||Ψ}}{{c||T}}), no. 40 (Toiano, {{c||T}}),
*from Golaseccan context {{bib|Stjernquist 1967}} no. 101:1 (Castelletto Ticino, {{c||50 (character)}}),
*from Raetic context {{bib|Stjernquist 1967}} no. 18:1 (Sanzeno, {{c||I}}{{c||T}} = [https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/SZ-82 TIR SZ-82]), no. 17 (Eppan/Appiano, {{c||T}}, lines and an asterisk), no. 16 (Moritzing/San Maurizio, twice a chevron surrounded by small strokes = [https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/BZ-7 TIR BZ-7]).
Further comparanda from Raetic context are: *a situla from Moritzing/San Maurizio ([https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/BZ-8 TIR BZ-8]) with two marks {{c||U}}{{c||I}}{{c||U}}{{c||I}} and {{c||I}} {{c||I}} {{c||line d 21}} {{c||I}} {{c||line d 21}} {{c||I}} on the rim, *a fragmented situla from Moritzing/San Maurizio ([https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/Non-script_notational_systems TIR] EX-67) with unidentifiable characters on the rim, *a situla from Sanzeno ([https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/SZ-36 TIR SZ-36]) with {{c||I}}{{c||Ψ}}{{c||T}}{{c||I}} on the rim, *a situla from Sanzeno ([https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/Non-script_notational_systems TIR] EX-37) with marks (shapes not specified) on the rim, *a fragment of the rim of a bronze vessel from Cles ([https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/Non-script_notational_systems TIR] EX-55) with {{c|line_d2_1}}{{c||I}} {{c||I}}{{c||I}}{{c||I}}{{c||I}} {{c||T}}{{c||I}}{{c||I}}{{c||I}}, *a fragmentary bronze vessel from Mechel/Meclo ([https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/NO-8 TIR NO-8]) with {{c||P}}{{c||I}} on the rim. See [https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/Non-script_notational_systems TIR] for similar marks on bucket handles (including the one mentioned by Mees p. 175) – the large number of examples from the Raetic area (Fritzens-Sanzeno culture) may be due to the good state of research on para-script in this area. The Raetic inscription corpus is also the only one to provide language-encoding inscriptions on the rims of situlae from North Italic context: [https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/HU-7 HU-7] (Situla in Providence, ca. 525, find place uncertain) and [https://www.univie.ac.at/raetica/wiki/CE-1 CE-1] (Situla Giovanelli, Cembra, 4th c.). Both the latter documents are lengthy (27 and 59 letters respectively) and unambiguously legible and interpretable; while it is true that the sequence on the Pansdorf cist is long compared to the 1–2 character marks on the cists listed by Stjernquist, and epsilon is not one of the standard characters to turn up in the average para-script sequence of crosses, chevrons and strokes, Pansdorf bears greater resemblance to those marks than to any language-encoding inscriptions. Whether Pellegrini's tentative interpretation of the sequence as a measurement is correct must be left open until our understanding of North Italic numeral writing improves. Considering the standard forms of Etruscan ciphers, the characters when interpreted as number signs are maybe better read as seen from the outside of the cist {{c|E|E6}}{{c|1 (character)}}{{c|1 (character)}}{{c|1 (character)}}{{c|5 (character)|5 (character)2}}{{c|50 (character)}}{{c|line_d2_1}}, even though in this case epsilon is inverted. In any case, the sequence on the Pansdorf pail does lend itself to an interpretation as a tally-like number, which is more than can be said for similar sequences from Raetic context, for which no system of number writing has so far been made plausible as the characters tend to appear in sequences that are not suggestive of a tally-like system like the Etruscan one. On the other hand, there are numerous examples of possible and plausible number writing in the Cisalpine Celtic context (see [[Numbers]]).

==Commentary==
Images in {{bib|Mangani 1986}}: 278 (drawing) and tav. XLII.2 (photo).

Inscribed below the rim of the cup between the two handles; a section of the text (about seven letters) is missing with a fragment of the cup. Measurements from {{bib|Mangani 1986}}: 278, no. 3. Mangani says that the decoration was made before firing, which seems to include the inscription. Cf. the similar inscription [[AS 3.1]] on an identical object from the same grave.

The text is a standard Etruscan dedicatory speaking inscription. The personal name {{w||keivale}}, preserved completely in [[AS 3.1]], is grammatically Etruscan, but is suggested to be "di origine forse non etrusca" by {{bib|Mangani 1986}}: 278 on account of the apparent suffix -''al''-, which she associates with Raetic, Ligurian and/or Lepontic; see further on the word page. AS 3.2 features an additional, but fragmentary word, probably a second name on the genitive (see {{bib|Mangani 1986}}: 278 f.).

See also {{bib|Gambari & Colonna 1988}}: 154, {{bib|Colonna 1998}}: 263, {{bib|Sassatelli 2008}}: 338.

*Stephanie A. Layton, Etruscan Bucchero Pottery from Cetamura del Chianti (Gaiole), in Etruscan Studies 12.1 (2009)
